QF.id: IV Introduction to Interest Rate Derivatives_14

answer.text: You can take exposure without full cash outlay
Leverage is unique to equity derivatives

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Leverage in derivatives means the ability to buy/sell the underlying without fully paying for it immediately. You pay only a margin (fraction of the contract value), giving exposure to a much larger position.
**The Trap:** Leverage amplifies both gains AND losses — it doesn't guarantee increased returns. It's not unique to equity; all derivatives (including interest rate) provide leverage.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Leverage is a defining characteristic of derivatives tested across all NISM derivative exams (Series I, IV, VIII, XIII, XVI).
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** A 10% margin means 10x leverage. If the underlying moves 1%, your P&L is 10% of margin. This makes risk management through proper position sizing absolutely critical.
